text:
Buzzberg Daily Alpha.

We scanned feed across X, YouTube and Reddit, Substack .

The cleanest signal was rotation into “next liquid proxies” after AI semis: space, defense drones, humanoid robotics, Korea AI capex, and select compute infrastructure.

1. Space / defense kept attracting the highest-quality flow.

$RKLB  was the strongest space narrative. The thesis is no longer just launches. Speakers framed Rocket Lab as the only public, full-stack space pure play, with the Iridium/spectrum angle creating optionality beyond D2D: IoT, maritime, telemetry, PNT, drones and defense.

$ASTS  was tied to J-LEO subsidy speculation and broader space momentum. $ONDS was framed as a long-term drone/defense compounder, with near-term dilution/warrant risk but Russell inclusion and possible strategic buyer optionality. Drone baskets like $UMAC/$KTOS/$AVAV also came up as “future of warfare” watchlist names.

2. Humanoid robotics moved beyond hype into public-market vehicles.

$CCXI  / Agility Robotics became the main robotics discussion. Bulls like the first US-listed humanoid pure-play angle, Amazon/Nvidia backing, ~$300M backlog, H2 2026 production scaling and RaaS unit economics.

The caution: some speakers openly dislike the SPAC quality/valuation but still expect a speculative run because the public-market wrapper is scarce.

$BOT also came up as a liquid way to own private robotics markups over the next 12-18 months.

3. Korea AI capex was the most important YouTube/macro signal.

Bloomberg and Korean YouTube focused on South Korea’s massive AI infrastructure plan: Samsung/SK Hynix, chip fabs, data centers, renewables and power equipment. The read-through is broad: memory, equipment, power grid, shipbuilding, and domestic Korean suppliers.

Twitter translated that into $MU/$SNDK/DRAM upside overnight. Korean channels also highlighted power equipment and shipbuilders as beneficiaries of AI data-center power shortages and government-backed capex.

4. AI compute relative value is back.

$PENG had the most interesting under-the-radar thesis: possible Middle East sovereign AI breadcrumbs through SK Telecom, MWC 2026 meetings, and Blackwell cluster integration.

$CRWV vs $NBIS was framed as a relative-value trade: CRWV allegedly trades at a large discount to NBIS on 2030 deployed-capacity revenue assumptions, while leverage concerns should fade if revenue scales.

$MSFT also reappeared as a “capex misunderstood” long: Reddit and Twitter both argued the market is penalizing AI buildout the same way it misread Amazon’s reinvestment cycle.

5. Select semis are separating from the weak photonics tape.

Broad optics/photonics was still treated cautiously, but $MXL and $QCOM stood out.

$MXL: Panther/OpenZFS validation at Los Alamos was cited as real proof for the CPU/storage offload thesis.

$QCOM: speakers highlighted automotive design wins, data-center revenue ramp, and HBC/near-memory compute as a lower-power inference architecture.

6. Single-name alpha:

$ABVX was the cleanest biotech catalyst: speakers argued the cancer/NMSC safety concern did not replicate, the label is cleaner, and UC efficacy supports a rerate.

$FCEL stayed alive as a speculative carbon-capture setup tied to the Exxon Rotterdam pilot. One speaker sold tactically to rotate into robotics, but the core FCEL thesis was not described as broken.

$BB had a strong QNX/software thread: autos, robotics, industrial automation, Canada/Japan trade mission deals, and high-margin software model.

$SMCI was the clearest avoid: Taiwan raids, chip-smuggling probe, accounting history, auditor issues. The message was simple: governance risk overwhelms AI exposure.

$UMAC Unusual Machines, $RCAT Red Cat Holdings and $LASE Laser Photonics continue to ride the US drone manufacturing policy push.

The Trump administration's program to fund US drone makers (cutting reliance on Chinese DJI components) is now extending into defense procurement. Laser Photonics' anti-drone system was selected by the US Department of War earlier this month.

Yeah France has a good squad but have you heard of the bottleneck bros?

Unbeatable team ⚡️

text:
$SIVE AGM & Q1 2026 – The board upgrade that signals M&A readiness right now
 I’ve been reflecting on the AGM outcome and the Q1 transcript, and one thread stands out more clearly than before: this company is deliberately positioning itself for the next layer of acceleration.
 Vickram was direct about it:

“This brings a very experienced and balanced board composition for us, and in particular, tremendous executive credibility and experience with U.S. markets, as well as strong M&A experience as we look not only at organically growing our business, but also adjacencies, complementary portfolios as well for the future if that can help further accelerate growth for the company.”

That’s not just some corporate line. That’s the CEO openly saying they’re keeping the door open for smart acquisitions.

The new board members feel spot on for this phase. Joakim Nideborn as Vice Chairman with his listed-company CFO experience and Helena Svancar with her deep M&A background (including time as Head of M&A at $ERIC) these are exactly the kind of people you want around the table when you’re thinking about scaling faster than organic growth alone can take you.

I’ve seen this story play out with $SIVEF before. Back in 2017 they acquired CST Global, what became Sivers Photonics. It was a bold bet on III-V lasers and the Glasgow foundry. There was dilution, integration headaches, and it took several years before the payoff became obvious. But look where we are today: that same platform is right in the middle of the AI data center laser ramps, LiDAR production ($AEVA), and the $JBL pluggable collaboration. It completely transformed what $SIVE could do.

MixComm followed a similar path, added real wireless depth and U.S. muscle, and now it’s helping drive the SATCOM momentum with https://t.co/A7mlfHMHLp / $YSS and the big IRIS² opportunity in Europe.
Both deals took time and patience, but they delivered real acceleration. I have a strong feeling we’re heading for something similar again. The setup just feels right: a nearly $800M pipeline that’s still growing fast, multiple 2027 ramps stacking up, and now a board with genuine M&A firepower. I’m actively digging into potential candidates right now, and when I’ve vetted something properly I’ll share my thoughts.
 What makes this moment particularly interesting is how the transcript frames the bigger picture. Vickram described being in the middle of three super cycles at once AI factories, space/SATCOM, and modern warfare and noted that $SIVE has differentiated technology that can meaningfully support elements of all three. He also highlighted the IRIS² European satellite infrastructure effort as a substantial long-term opportunity for the wireless business: a pipeline north of $80 million through 2030 (and that’s not even half the lifetime revenue potential), with $SIVE already engaged with five subcontractors in the final RFP stage for user terminals that start development in 2027. As an EU-based provider, they’re uniquely positioned on the sovereignty angle.
 The board changes, the explicit openness to adjacencies and M&A, the progress on U.S. dual-listing readiness, and the manufacturing capacity build-out all point in the same direction: $SIVE is building the foundation and the optionality to grow both organically and through smart additions at exactly the moment the secular tailwinds are strongest.
 I remain deeply convicted as a long-term shareholder. The transformation from development-led to product-led is progressing, the pipeline is a genuine lead indicator, and the company now has the governance and expertise in place to act on opportunities that can meaningfully accelerate the trajectory.
 Small volumes turning into bigger numbers and potentially even bigger ones, faster, with the right moves.

What are your thoughts? Did anything in the transcript or the board changes stand out to you? Or any areas you think would make sense for a potential acquisition? Would love to hear!

text:
My thesis was that stocks top with $SPCX.

Why?

Stock bros up 20x, crypto bros buying stock bros bags at ATH’s; the very same “retail” that was crushed at the top of every crypto bull cycle…

Everyone following stock KOL’s like @aleabitoreddit and ape whatever ticker is mentioned.

Trillion dollar IPO’s.

Literally the same sh** we’ve seen at the top of every crypto bull cycle.

Of course, the difference is these are real companies with real revenue, but timing does still matter unless you want to sit -30% under water for a couple years and HODL.

And at the end of the day, businesses fail, competition is fierce, there’s no way every single one of your bottleneck photonics glass substrate hydro-cooling picks are going to just keep going up over time.

Everyone’s a genius season- notice it when it’s there and start looking for the exit.

text:
$SIVE / $SIVEF Bull Case: Why Sivers Semiconductors could become the next $LITE

(Disclaimer-I have a position in Sivers)

Everyone wants the next GPU winner.

Few are looking at the companies enabling AI infrastructure behind the scenes.

That’s the Sivers thesis.

The opportunity isn’t becoming another Nvidia—it’s becoming part of the connectivity layer that AI depends on.

As AI clusters scale, compute is no longer the only bottleneck. Moving massive amounts of data efficiently is becoming just as important. More bandwidth, lower latency, lower power.

That’s where optical networking and photonics enter.

Sivers is positioning around:
• Optical engines
• External laser solutions
• Co-packaged optics (CPO)
• AI datacenter connectivity

The reason bulls compare it to $LITE:

Lumentum became valuable because optical components quietly became mission critical.

If AI infrastructure follows a similar path, the companies enabling data movement—not just compute—could see outsized value creation.

Sivers has also built relationships across the ecosystem through collaborations and development efforts with major manufacturing and photonics players. That doesn’t guarantee success—but it creates a path to relevance.

The bet here isn’t that Sivers dominates AI.

The bet is that it captures a small but important piece of a market growing exponentially.

Small company.
Big TAM.
Execution required.

That’s where asymmetric setups come from.

That doesn’t guarantee success—but it creates a path to relevance. The bet here isn’t that Sivers dominates AI. The bet is that it captures a small but important piece of a market growing exponentially. Small company. Big TAM. Execution required. text:
If you're not buying today there's probably no reason to be checking your portfolio every five minutes
Close the app and go outside

It's always refreshing being around people in the real world who have absolutely no idea the market is down

I don't think this is the start of a bear market or some epic crash. If anything, the market seems to be getting a little ahead of itself on rate cut expectations

$MU reports tonight and I think it'll be one of the more important reads for the AI and photonics trade

Volatility is the price you pay for superior returns
It sucks but that's the game we play

text:
The following is an objective and comprehensive analysis of $NOK, $MRVL, and $MSFT, based on the latest publicly available financial reports and reliable sources (as of around June 24, 2026; the most recent quarter for NOK/MRVL/MSFT is their respective FY period; some Q2/Q3 updates are available).

1. $NOK (Nokia Corporation)

Company Overview: A global leader in telecommunications network infrastructure, with businesses covering mobile networks (5G/6G), optical networks, IP routing, and enterprise solutions. It is transforming into AI infrastructure (photonics, data center connectivity).

Main Revenue Sources: Network infrastructure; AI/cloud customer revenue has grown rapidly in recent years (increasing its share in the most recent quarter).

Competitive Advantages: Accumulated 5G/optical network technology, global operator relationships, and the acquisition of Infinera enhancing photonics capabilities; outstanding cost-effectiveness in the AI ​​data center interconnect field.

Revenue: Approximately $20 billion TTM, with AI-related growth of 49% in some quarters.

Profit Margin: Improved gross margin (approximately 45.5% recently), net margin approximately 4%.

FCF/Liabilities: Stable cash flow and manageable debt.

Valuation: Trailing P/E ~85x (relatively high), Forward P/E ~36x, P/S ~3.3x. Reasonable compared to historical cycles, but with a significant premium from AI transformation; attractive growth compared to telecom peers.

Industry/Sector Position: Telecom infrastructure sector, currently showing divergent performance driven by AI. The company has a solid position in 5G/photonics, and the sector trend is shifting towards AI data center interconnection.

Recent News and Catalysts (last 1-4 weeks):

Positive: Trump mentions NOK, expanded AI cooperation with Amazon/Google, growth in AI infrastructure orders.

Negative: Cyclical fluctuations in telecom spending, overall market correction pressure.

Impact: Short-term (1-3 months) significant earnings catalyst; long-term (6-12 months) accelerated growth if AI transformation is implemented effectively.

Risks and Opportunities:

Risks: Telecom capital expenditure cycle, intensified competition, execution risks.

Opportunities: AI photonics orders, 5G/6G deployment, successful transformation.

Overall Assessment: Valuation is reasonably high (forward valuation is reasonable, trailing reflects transformation expectations). Bull Case: AI infrastructure orders exceeded expectations, and profit margins continue to expand. Bear Case: Telecom spending slowed, and transformation fell short of expectations.

2. $MRVL (Marvell Technology)

Company Overview: A semiconductor company focusing on data centers, custom AI chips, electro-optical interconnects, and enterprise networks.

Main Revenue Sources: Data centers (AI-driven, significantly increasing to 75%+), custom silicon, and electro-optical products.

Competitive Advantages: Deep collaboration with NVIDIA, custom AI ASIC design capabilities, and leading data center interconnect technology.

Financial Health (FY2026 Data):

Revenue: Record $8.195 billion in FY2026 (YoY +42%); strong growth in recent quarters such as Q2 and FY2027.

Profit Margin: Non-GAAP gross margin ~59%, significantly improved.

FCF: Strong operating cash flow.

Valuation: P/S/P/E elevated due to high growth (depending on the latest price, market cap is in the tens of billions of dollars). Significantly improved compared to historical levels, competitive with AI semiconductor peers.

Industry/Sector Position: Semiconductor/AI Chip Sector, currently fluctuating. The company's position in the data center AI chip/interconnect field is rising; the sector trend is driven by AI capex but is experiencing a short-term pullback.

Recent News and Catalysts:

Positive: Strong AI demand, NVIDIA collaboration, upward revision of revenue guidance.

Negative: Overall selling pressure in the sector, high valuation correction.

Impact: Short-term earnings/order catalysts; long-term growth in custom AI silicon.

Risks and Opportunities:

Risks: Slowing AI spending, competition, valuation compression.

Opportunities: Custom AI chip orders, data center expansion.

Overall Assessment: Valuation reflects high growth, reasonably high (AI premium). Bull Case: Continued outperformance in AI orders, revenue acceleration. Bear Case: Slower cycle or increased competition leading to lower-than-expected growth.

3. $MSFT (Microsoft Corporation)

Company Overview: Technology giant, with businesses covering cloud (Azure), software (Office), AI (Copilot/OpenAI), games, etc.

Key Revenue Streams: Intelligent Cloud (dominated by Azure), Productivity & Business Processes.

Competitive Advantages: Cloud market leadership, AI integration (Copilot), enterprise ecosystem lock-in, OpenAI collaboration.

Financial Health (FY2026 Q3 data):

Revenue: Q3 $82.9 billion (YoY +18%), Cloud $54.5 billion (+29%).

Profit Margin: Strong, with significant AI contribution.

FCF: Excellent, but CapEx is high (AI infrastructure).

Valuation: Reasonable Trailing/Forward P/E (growth stock premium), robust P/S. Attractive compared to historical levels and peers (technology cloud giants).

Industry/Sector Position: Cloud computing/AI software sector, currently performing steadily. The company is a cloud/AI leader, and the sector trend is shifting towards AI integration.

Recent News and Catalysts:

Positive Factors: High-speed growth in Cloud/AI revenue (AI annualized return of over 37 billion), Copilot adoption.

Negative Factors: High CapEx (AI investment), concerns about a slight slowdown in Azure growth.

Impact: Short-term earnings-driven; long-term AI monetization accelerated.

Risks and Opportunities:

Risks: Slow AI returns, CapEx pressure, competition.

Opportunities: Copilot monetization, expanding cloud market share, AI ecosystem.

Overall Assessment: Reasonable valuation (supported by growth). Bull Case: AI/Copilot exceeds expectations, Cloud accelerates. Bear Case: Delayed AI investment returns, slower growth.

Summary: All three companies deeply benefit from AI, but NOK is more geared towards telecom transformation, MRVL has high AI semiconductor purity, and MSFT is a leading player in both cloud and AI. Data timeliness is based on the latest financial reports; it is recommended to pay attention to their next financial reports. Market risks exist; the above is only an objective analysis.

text:
Bright Solutions' COTS laser platforms; designed to support mission-critical applications:

https://t.co/Fx7Q4MKzyN]$

#LaserSystems #Photonics #LiDAR #DefenseTech #Engineering #LaserInnovation #BrightSolutions #RPMCLasers https://t.co/z6qWmvxw2n

https://t.co/0ncKgB2wBv

This is exactly the kind of experiment more people should be paying attention to.

Scientists used water waves to make an otherwise “invisible” quantum-style effect visible. A vortex in a tank acted as an analogue to the magnetic-flux structure in the Aharonov–Bohm effect. The waves did not need to pass through the vortex core for the global wave pattern to change.

That is the key point.

A hidden topological structure altered the phase of the wave environment, and the result became visible as system-wide nodal lines. Even more interesting: as the vortex strength increased, the number of rotating nodal lines became quantized.

To me, the deeper lesson is not “water proves quantum mechanics.”

It is that organized wave environments can reveal invisible structure through global diagnostic patterns.

Topology became visible.

Phase became visible.

A local organizing feature produced a non-local wave signature.

That matters far beyond this one experiment.

For ZPF Technologies, this is close to the language we are trying to formalize: dynamically organized electromagnetic environments should not only be analyzed by field strength or energy density. They should also be studied through phase, topology, boundary conditions, mode accessibility, noise structure, sidebands, linewidths, and residuals after standard explanations are subtracted.

If water waves can reveal hidden topological structure through nodal patterns, then advanced electromagnetic systems deserve the same kind of diagnostic imagination.

The frontier is not just building stronger fields.

It is learning how organized environments leave organized observables.

That is where topology, phase, stochastic dynamics, quantum vacuum diagnostics, photonics, plasma systems, and future fusion research may begin to converge.
#Quantum #quantumphysics #QuantumEngineering

text:
2026 PORTFOLIO UPDATE  Performance Metrics 

June 2026     :    6.39%
Harris YTD     : 45.79% 
S&P500 YTD: 12.84%  

My portfolio currently has 9 stocks: $nbis, $sofi, $mu, $aaoi, $asts, $oust, $pltr, $nflx, $onds

Currently 6/9 stocks in green, lagging only $sofi (who else!), $aaoi and $nflx.

Month Recap: It wasn't an easy month. It had a lot of ups and downs, and I'm really surprised that we closed the month in green. At one point I was -9%, but with the huge pump the last few days of the month, we managed to close in profit.

I will write down some thoughts of mine per each holding.
$nbis: This is the stock I have high conviction on. That doesn't mean I didn't trade it. I sold and bought quite a lot of times to make some profits and to initiate other positions. I've held it for one year now.
$sofi: I hold it from last year. Still in red, but I managed to bring my average down to $18.7, so I'm pretty close to breaking even. I love their CEO (@anthonynoto, send me a hat) as I believe he makes an extremely good job. The company is progressing really well, and the stock price will follow eventually.
$mu: I used to have SK Hynix, and I switched to have exposure to US markets. That's all. Same philosophy. Memory is in deep need.
$aaoi: A big player in photonics. All sector had a bad month. It will recover (I'm -14%), I'm not afraid.
$asts: I started buying when $spcx launched all the way to the bottom. I'm in green, I have a good average, and I believe a lot. Nothing more to say.
$oust: You see what happened last days with Physical AI and robots. Initially I bought in Aprin around $23 and sold at $36 for quick profit. Now I'm long here. Huge profit atm, I expect a pullback to take a breather.
$pltr & $nflx: I wanted exposure to both companies and I was looking for the opportunity. When $pltr dropped below 200week ema, I bought it and I have averaged. Netflix (last stock in red) gives me more opportunities for DCAing.
$onds: It was on my radar. I have issues with their CEO and his tactics (dilution and pumping), but at $7.9 it was a bargain and there was a huge disconnect between the company and the stock price. It's not a long-term position. When it reaches $10-$12, I planned to trim it.

Thanks for staying up to the end! If you enjoyed it, drop a 👋 emoji to see who read it.

Consider following me in order to be together in that journey. Today I reached 100k portfolio, so that's a huge milestone to celebrate!

text:
Half of the year is overwith. 

I have been fairly inactive over the last month as I spend time reflecting on the year, analyzing my holdings and seeing new opportunities for 2H. 

If I compartmentalize the year by months, I outperformed the majority in months 1-3 by having a defensive portfolio 25’ that was primary legacy manufacturers like $vrt $cat $gev. From April -> May I slightly underperformed, by not being able to pivot fast enough into emerging themes like memory and semis, only getting exposure to photonics through $aaoi. 

May -> present was OK, although my heavy positioning in $spcx ancillaries pulled performance down. Looking ahead I see no reason to 2H 26’ to be any different than what we have seen for the last few years. 

As I’m writing this I see Burry is now short my second largest holding caterpillar and I kindly ask him to accept my ballbag as I maintain bullish on the resurgence of heavy machinery and manufacturing names amidst an extremely pro America/infrastructure administration.

text:
Here's the post:

If I could only watch four layers in optical interconnect, ranked by bottleneck severity:
$COHR $LITE $FN $ANET

Nvidia just spent $6 billion locking down optical supply between March and June, and the market still thinks this is about transceivers.

The real story is InP substrates and laser chips — the stuff 20 layers upstream from what most people are tracking. Here's how I'd rank the supply chain by choke point plus earnings visibility over the next 12-24 months.

1. InP materials and epitaxy — the actual valve on compute scale. $COHR and $LITE aren't just CPO plays. They control high-purity indium phosphide epitaxy and high-speed laser IP that physically cannot be replaced in 800G/1.6T modules. Intel's CEO said it outright: InP is the non-negotiable material for solving chip-to-chip bandwidth. Nvidia locked each of them with separate $2 billion commitments and long-term offtake agreements. The Street prices them as module vendors. They're actually material oligopolists with compounding pricing power through every speed iteration.

2. CPO laser engines — the leverage on every GPU watt. $LITE and $COHR again, but now wearing their co-packaged optics hat. CPO moves the photonics inside the package, right next to the switch ASIC or GPU. That requires advanced packaging plus high-power lasers that don't cook themselves. Supply is sewn up. Goldman just put the scale-up interconnect TAM at $154 billion, and most of that growth is inside the rack and inside the super-pod, not between data centers. CPO sits in the middle of that build-out. Earnings inflection is 2026-2028 when hyperscalers move from pilots to volume. Risk is simple: if Nvidia capex wobbles, these names will gap hard because the trade is already crowded.

3. Module assembly and high-mix manufacturing — where cash flow actually prints. $FN is the TSMC of optical: low-drama contract manufacturing for every major module and CPO vendor. Margins are thinner than the chip guys, but revenue visibility is the cleanest in the whole chain because hyperscale deployment schedules are locked 18 months out. TrendForce just lifted 2025-2026 AI transceiver revenue from $16.5 billion to $26 billion, up 57% year-over-year. FN captures that with zero demand risk and no inventory. It's the only name here where you can model the next eight quarters with a straight face.

4. Switch and networking integration — the layer everyone already owns. $ANET is the consensus optical AI trade. Data center switching tied to optical back-end is a known quantity. Multiples reflect it. I'd rather own the three above because the market hasn't priced the material and assembly bottleneck the same way it's priced the switch attach rate.

The non-consensus bit: A-share money has been rotating from NAND and legacy semis into InP names for two quarters, but U.S. allocators still think optical is a 2025 story. It's a 2026-2028 margin story, and the earnings gap between upstream material plays and downstream module integrators is about to widen hard. I'm overweight the top two on that list until CPO production data forces multiple expansion.

Computational Ghost Imaging reconstructs high-resolution images using just a single-pixel detector—opening new possibilities for X-ray imaging, biomedical research, and beyond.

text:
This 🎯roadmap🎯 for data-center interconnect speeds is also, in many ways, a roadmap for #siliconphotonics.  As data rates move higher, the industry will need more integrated optical solutions, including near-package optics and co-packaged optics. That is where #siliconphotonics becomes increasingly relevant.
I adapted this roadmap based on analysis from an investment bank, which states that it is built on company disclosures as well as its own research.
It is interesting to observe how the upgrade cycles are overlapping. According to this analysis 400G is already moving into decline, 800G is becoming the mainstream workhorse, 1.6T is entering mass-volume territory, and 3.2T is already visible before 1.6T has fully settled. Furthermore, 800G may remain mainstream for only around six quarters. That is shorter than the typical 2–4 year cadence often expected for a mainstream optical transceiver generation. This points to a compressed innovation cadence for the industry. Qualification, yield learning, supply-chain readiness, thermal design, packaging, DSP, and test infrastructure all need to mature faster than before. Only the 💪fittest can survive this race. My view is that integrated photonics is entering a sustained innovation cycle, driven by the bandwidth, power, density, and packaging pressures of AI data-center infrastructure. 
🚀 #siliconphotonics

text: 6G's whole pitch rides on terahertz, and terahertz is brutal to actually build antennas for. This new compact "topological" antenna (in Nature Photonics) handles those info-dense THz signals — exactly the unglamorous breakthrough that makes the flashy 6G demos possible. @NaturePhotonics @jeffkagan https://t.co/zwoCLtnzoy

text:
Russia ITMO University: Optical Tractor Beam with Metasurfaces and United States: Boeing's Magnetic Tractor Beam Patent

#ufotwitter

It's fascinating milestone in the field of modern nanophotonics. Published in ACS Photonics in late 2018 by physicists from ITMO University, the research details how hyperbolic metasurfaces can be used to generate stable optical pulling forces—commonly referred to as an "optical tractor beam."

To understand how this functions, it helps to look at the mechanics of standard radiation pressure and contrast it with how these specialized, engineered surfaces flip that mechanic on its head.

The Problem with Normal Light

Under ordinary conditions, when a laser beam strikes a small dielectric particle, the photons transfer their linear momentum to the object. This creates a standard scattering or radiation pressure that pushes the particle away from the light source, along the direction of propagation.

Creating a "tractor beam" requires generating a negative optical force (a pulling force) that acts against the global momentum of the incoming light, drawing the particle backward toward the source.

How Hyperbolic Metasurfaces Change the Mechanics

A hyperbolic metasurface is an ultra-thin, artificially engineered material characterized by extreme anisotropy. This means its electromagnetic properties (specifically its permittivity, have different signs along different spatial axes—for example, acting like a metal  in one direction and like a dielectric in another.

When a small particle is placed close to this surface and illuminated by a standard unstructured light source (like a plane wave), the interaction relies on three core phenomena:

High Density of States & Asymmetric Scattering: The hyperbolic nature of the substrate opens up highly localized, high-density electromagnetic interaction channels (both surface plasmons and volumetric waves within the substrate). The presence of the particle scatterer near the interface selectively excites these asymmetrical near-field modes.

Momentum Conservation and Recoil: Because the substrate allows light to scatter preferentially into these highly confined, high-momentum modes directed forward along or into the surface, the law of conservation of linear momentum requires a balance. To compensate for the forward momentum of the tailored scattering channels, the particle experiences a net mechanical recoil in the opposite direction.

Broadband Pulling: Unlike older optical trapping configurations that require complex, actively modulated phase holograms or spatial light modulators (SLMs) to sculpt a specific beam geometry (like a solenoid or Bessel beam), this model utilizes the intrinsic dispersion of the substrate itself. This allows for stable optical pulling forces over a significantly broader spectral range using simpler incident light fields.

Why This Mattered

Prior to this approach, pulling particles with light required highly complex, bulky optical setups that restricted spatial movement and demanded high laser power, which could damage biological samples or nanoscale structures. By shifting the engineering challenge from the light source to the material environment (the metasurface), this framework opened up new avenues for nanoscale manipulation, chip-based sorting of cells or macromolecules, and nanoscale fluidic self-organization.

United States: Boeing's Magnetic Tractor Beam Patent

This is an interesting find. You’re looking at US Patent US6634603B2, titled "Magnetic dipole tractor beam control system", filed by The Boeing Company back in 2001 and granted in 2003.

While the phrase "tractor beam" instantly evokes sci-fi, the actual engineering framework here is grounded in classic electromagnetic field manipulation. Here is a breakdown of what this patent actually describes, how it works, and its intended application.

The Core Concept: How It Works

Unlike fictional tractor beams that use exotic energy to pull objects across massive distances, this system relies on the predictable physics of magnetic dipole interactions.

If you have two magnetic dipoles (which can be generated using electromagnets or superconducting loops), they exert a force and a torque on one another based on their relative orientation, distance, and field strength.

The patent describes a system designed to:

Attract or Repel: Manipulate the magnetic fields to pull a target object closer or push it away.

Control Orientation: Adjust the torque to precisely rotate, stabilize, or align the target object in three-dimensional space without physical contact.

Dynamic Feedback: Continuously measure the position and orientation of the target, feeding that data into a control processor to adjust the magnetic fields in real-time, preventing the target from drifting or snapping violently out of position.

The Intended Application

In space, docking two multi-ton vehicles (like a shuttle or capsule connecting to a space station) is incredibly risky. Mechanical docking mechanisms require physical impact, which can transmit dangerous kinetic energy, cause structural fatigue, or send both craft into an uncontrolled tumble if misaligned.

The "Tractor Beam" Solution:

By using this magnetic dipole control system, a chaser spacecraft could:

Approach a passive target satellite or station module.

Activate the controlled magnetic fields from a safe distance.

Gently capture, damp out any residual spin (tumble) of the target, and guide it into a precise mechanical lock without ever touching it until the final capture.

It's a highly sophisticated, non-contact magnetic tethering and dampening system—essentially a high-tech "soft-capture" mechanism for orbital logistics.

#ufotwitter

It's fascinating milestone in the field of modern nanophotonics. Published in ACS Photonics in late 2018 by physicists from ITMO University, the research details how hyperbolic metasurfaces can be used to generate stable optical pulling forces—commonly referred to as an "optical tractor beam."

To understand how this functions, it helps to look at the mechanics of standard radiation pressure and contrast it with how these specialized, engineered surfaces flip that mechanic on its head.

The Problem with Normal Light

Under ordinary conditions, when a laser beam strikes a small dielectric particle, the photons transfer their linear momentum to the object. This creates a standard scattering or radiation pressure that pushes the particle away from the light source, along the direction of propagation.

Creating a "tractor beam" requires generating a negative optical force (a pulling force) that acts against the global momentum of the incoming light, drawing the particle backward toward the source.

How Hyperbolic Metasurfaces Change the Mechanics

A hyperbolic metasurface is an ultra-thin, artificially engineered material characterized by extreme anisotropy. This means its electromagnetic properties (specifically its permittivity, have different signs along different spatial axes—for example, acting like a metal  in one direction and like a dielectric in another.

When a small particle is placed close to this surface and illuminated by a standard unstructured light source (like a plane wave), the interaction relies on three core phenomena:

High Density of States & Asymmetric Scattering: The hyperbolic nature of the substrate opens up highly localized, high-density electromagnetic interaction channels (both surface plasmons and volumetric waves within the substrate). The presence of the particle scatterer near the interface selectively excites these asymmetrical near-field modes.

Momentum Conservation and Recoil: Because the substrate allows light to scatter preferentially into these highly confined, high-momentum modes directed forward along or into the surface, the law of conservation of linear momentum requires a balance. To compensate for the forward momentum of the tailored scattering channels, the particle experiences a net mechanical recoil in the opposite direction.

Broadband Pulling: Unlike older optical trapping configurations that require complex, actively modulated phase holograms or spatial light modulators (SLMs) to sculpt a specific beam geometry (like a solenoid or Bessel beam), this model utilizes the intrinsic dispersion of the substrate itself. This allows for stable optical pulling forces over a significantly broader spectral range using simpler incident light fields.

Why This Mattered

Prior to this approach, pulling particles with light required highly complex, bulky optical setups that restricted spatial movement and demanded high laser power, which could damage biological samples or nanoscale structures. By shifting the engineering challenge from the light source to the material environment (the metasurface), this framework opened up new avenues for nanoscale manipulation, chip-based sorting of cells or macromolecules, and nanoscale fluidic self-organization.

United States: Boeing's Magnetic Tractor Beam Patent

This is an interesting find. You’re looking at US Patent US6634603B2, titled "Magnetic dipole tractor beam control system", filed by The Boeing Company back in 2001 and granted in 2003.

While the phrase "tractor beam" instantly evokes sci-fi, the actual engineering framework here is grounded in classic electromagnetic field manipulation. Here is a breakdown of what this patent actually describes, how it works, and its intended application.

The Core Concept: How It Works

Unlike fictional tractor beams that use exotic energy to pull objects across massive distances, this system relies on the predictable physics of magnetic dipole interactions.

If you have two magnetic dipoles (which can be generated using electromagnets or superconducting loops), they exert a force and a torque on one another based on their relative orientation, distance, and field strength.

The patent describes a system designed to:

Attract or Repel: Manipulate the magnetic fields to pull a target object closer or push it away.

Control Orientation: Adjust the torque to precisely rotate, stabilize, or align the target object in three-dimensional space without physical contact.

Dynamic Feedback: Continuously measure the position and orientation of the target, feeding that data into a control processor to adjust the magnetic fields in real-time, preventing the target from drifting or snapping violently out of position.

The Intended Application

In space, docking two multi-ton vehicles (like a shuttle or capsule connecting to a space station) is incredibly risky. Mechanical docking mechanisms require physical impact, which can transmit dangerous kinetic energy, cause structural fatigue, or send both craft into an uncontrolled tumble if misaligned.

The "Tractor Beam" Solution:

By using this magnetic dipole control system, a chaser spacecraft could:

Approach a passive target satellite or station module.

Activate the controlled magnetic fields from a safe distance.

Gently capture, damp out any residual spin (tumble) of the target, and guide it into a precise mechanical lock without ever touching it until the final capture.

It's a highly sophisticated, non-contact magnetic tethering and dampening system—essentially a high-tech "soft-capture" mechanism for orbital logistics.

#ufotwitter

Kepler Aerospace is a Texas-based company positioning itself as an "Exotic Propulsion and Exotic Energy Company." Their stated focus spans several advanced and unconventional areas, including:

Propulsion & Energy: They claim to work on proprietary technologies such as exotic electromagnetic propulsion, compact mobile fusion, and "gravity modification" or "gravitational shielding."

Terrestrial Applications: Beyond space flight, they are developing systems intended to process fossil fuels into cleaner-burning aviation and diesel fuels.

Divisions: The company operates through several specialized units, including Manufacturing, Astrodynamics, Electronics, Geophysics, and a Military division focused on security technologies.

The company leadership includes Dr. John Brandenburg as Chief Science Officer. Their public-facing presence highlights an interest in both commercial and military applications, with some activities centered on aerospace training and development in the West Texas region.

Kepler Aerospace positions itself at the intersection of conventional aerospace manufacturing and high-risk, unconventional physics. Here are the key takeaways regarding their technical focus and mission:

Core Technical Mandate: The company explicitly identifies as an "Exotic Propulsion and Exotic Energy Company." They state they are pursuing technologies that move beyond standard aerospace limitations, specifically citing compact mobile fusion, exotic electromagnetic propulsion, and gravity modification/shielding.

Dual-Use Strategic Focus: Their business model is divided into two distinct lanes:

Space/Defense: Leveraging advanced, potentially paradigm-shifting physics for astrodynamics and military security applications.

Terrestrial Energy: Developing processes to convert fossil fuels into cleaner-burning aviation and diesel fuels, positioning this as a key sustainability and emissions-reduction product.

Scientific Leadership: Their technical direction is influenced by Dr. John Brandenburg, who serves as Chief Science Officer. Brandenburg is known for unconventional hypotheses, including his work suggesting evidence of large-scale thermonuclear explosions on Mars in the deep past.

Operational Footprint: They maintain a suite of divisions—Manufacturing, Astrodynamics, Electronics, Geophysics, and Military—and emphasize operations out of the West Texas region, often engaging in aerospace training and regional development initiatives.

Business Positioning: While they emphasize "commercially viable" development, their public profile leans heavily on high-concept research. They maintain a firm stance against critical outside scrutiny, as evidenced by recent public releases addressing and dismissing online allegations regarding the company.

Advisory Board
The company utilizes an advisory board consisting of figures associated with alternative physics, advanced engineering, and legacy defense programs:

Dr. Robert M. L Baker, Jr.: A foundational figure in modern astrodynamics who is heavily involved in high-frequency gravitational wave research.

Herman “David” Froning: An aerospace engineer noted for publishing extensive theoretical work on field propulsion, vacuum engineering, and laser-driven space flight.

John Cole & Dr. Frank Mead: Both have histories connected to advanced or breakthrough propulsion concepts within institutional or military frameworks.

Alexander Karimov: Kepler Aerospace Advisor

Alexander Karimov is an academic and researcher operating at the high end of Russian experimental and theoretical physics. His credentials include:

Doctor of Science (Physics and Mathematics, 1999): Obtained from the prestigious Moscow Engineering Physical Institute (MEPhI), a premier Soviet/Russian institution tightly bound to fundamental nuclear research and advanced military-industrial technology. He also completed his Ph.D. there in 1994.

Professor at MEPhI: Specifically within the Department of Electrophysical Installations, which deals directly with high-energy systems, particle accelerators, and plasma physics.

Leading Researcher at JIHT RAS: Since 1996, he has been a principal researcher at the Joint Institute for High Temperatures of the Russian Academy of Sciences (Division for Magneto-plasma aerodynamics and MHD energy conversion).

Karimov’s background bridges aggressive aerospace engineering concepts with fundamental energy physics. The bio highlights several highly specialized pillars:

Magnetohydrodynamic (MHD) Energy Conversion: This involves generating electrical power directly from moving plasma or high-velocity ionized gases without moving mechanical parts. In aerospace, MHD is heavily researched for advanced propulsion, hypersonic flow control (reducing drag or thermal loads on re-entry vehicles), and power extraction.

Magneto-plasma Aerodynamics: The study of how magnetic fields interact with ionized air flows (plasmas) around a vehicle. This has direct implications for stealth (plasma stealth fields), hyper-velocity flight, and atmospheric propulsion.

Laser, Beam, and Plasma Physics: Core disciplines involving high-energy directional beams and the manipulation of plasma states.

Hydrodynamic Coherent Structures: The text notes his specific work on the "Role of Initial Conditions in the Formation of Coherent Structures in Hydrodynamic Systems," an area of fluid mechanics critical to understanding how stable vortexes, plasmas, or fluid structures form and persist.

His Role at Kepler Aerospace

Plasma Products & Plasma Engineering: Utilizing his decades of research at the Russian Academy of Sciences to help design systems that manipulate or generate plasma fields.

text:
The Physical AI Thematic is moving really fast...
$OUST $AMBA $AEVA $SYNA $CGNX $VPG

Over the last few weeks, I put out two layer reports

> A deep dive into the perception layer
> A deep dive into the edge compute layer

The top 3 companies from each layer I presented:

> $AMBA $OUST $CGNX 
> $SYNA $CEVA $LSCC

So the way it works is 3 steps:

1 - I present the 'layer' as an overview like why it's important, how dollars are captured, the durability etc.

2 - Within this report I present the top 3 companies I believe are well positioned. A quick thesis / overview

3 - Once I decide upon the top candidates, I then do a full deep dive into every aspect of the company, along with the weightings I am interested in and the financial modeling around them

While this is a very exciting time for this theme, we are still very early. There is no rush on my end for positioning or research. 

I started covering Physical AI ~6 weeks ago and along with photonics, these are the two themes I am researching and covering in depth. 

If we can really identify the strongest companies, we can position with a multi-year mindset

I still have 7 more layers to present and lots of positions I still need to take

Join us if you are interested! 

https://t.co/Xv5BKKCwOR

text:
$INFQ recent contract / funding momentum:

U.S. Navy : $1M contract for QuIRC, Infleqtion’s quantum-inspired AI software for RF signal processing in contested environments.
DARPA HARQ: $2M contract to develop Multistaq, a software platform for heterogeneous quantum systems across multiple qubit types.
NASA JPL: $20M in contracts to date for the Quantum Gravity Gradiometer Pathfinder mission, where Infleqtion is helping build space-based quantum sensing tech.
U.S. Department of Commerce / CHIPS Office: proposed $100M funding LOI to accelerate U.S. neutral-atom quantum computing, quantum hardware, photonics, and full-stack system development.
Important distinction: the $100M CHIPS item is not a finalized contract yet, but it is proposed, milestone-based funding subject to final documents and approvals. 

My Friday brain says: small announced contract dollars today, but strong validation from Navy, DARPA, NASA, DOE, and Commerce. $INFQ is building government credibility in quantum computing, sensing, defense, and space.
You know I love me some space.

text:
$IONQ

A recent report by Kearney makes it clear that trapped ions are the most promising way of building commercially useful quantum computers.

The report discusses these architectures:

1/ Trapped ions

Highest fidelity but slower gate speeds

All-to-all connectivity leads to lower physical qubit overhead

Best from unit-economics perspective

Bottom line: It’s the most commercially viable quantum computers. You’ll get high quality data when using them but have to wait for your calculations longer.

2/ Superconducting qubits

Fast gate times

Higher physical qubit overhead than for trapped ions

But even under optimistic assumptions, scaling will make system costs unsustainable

Report actually calls this an “existential architectural threat”

Bottom line: There’s a good chance you’ll never see a useful quantum computer built on millions of superconducting qubits

3/ Neutral atoms

High fidelity

All-to-all connectivity leading to lower physical qubit overhead like for trapped ions

As promising as trapped-ion technology but real challenge is engineering: Calibration automation, system resets, and error correction

Bottom line: Definitely has potential to scale up but not clear how engineering challenges will be overcome

4/ Photonics

Combines two advantages: Room-temperature operation and compatibility with foundries currently building semiconductor chips

Biggest bet is modularity, which means you break quantum computers into small interconnected processing units

Biggest challenge is scaling because photons don’t naturally interact with each other and light is subject to losses

Bottom line: Not clear if this manufacturing leverage will help scale photonics.

The conclusion of the report speaks for itself:

“The architectures most likely to lead are therefore the ones that can repeatedly translate physics progress into operationally stable, cost-improving systems, because the market will ultimately reward not “qubits on a slide,” but sustained improvement in error-corrected capability per dollar.

Based on Kearney analysis in collaboration with leading quantum companies, industry and academic experts, trapped-ion quantum computers are early leaders to be the most cost-effective quantum computer at fault tolerant scale.”

Long $IONQ.

For a stock growing triple digits, that looks pretty rich. The whole photonics group got hit the last couple of weeks, and almost none of it was about the companies. Co-packaged optics delay scares spooked people, the Fed turned hawkish, the memory names cracked, and the high-beta optical stocks got dumped with everything else. Classic crowded trade unwinding. Nothing actually changed in the businesses. So look at what you are getting at $130. The market cap is around $10.6 billion. Management's own target for mid-2027 is about $471 million a month in data center revenue, or roughly $5.6 billion a year. That puts the stock at about 2x that target today. Slower photonics names trade richer than that on the numbers they have already done. And this is not a hope and a prayer. Full-year guidance is $1.1 billion, more than double last year. Q1 grew 51%. They have over $324 million in locked 800G and 1.6T orders. They are expanding laser capacity by around 350% by the end of 2027. Supply is expected to trail demand the whole way through. They start shipping their big 1.6T order on July 1. Rosenblatt sees $220. The one fair knock is that management has to actually hit the numbers, and they have been lumpy in the past. That is the real risk, and I am not going to pretend it away. But the tape is treating a triple-digit grower in the middle of the photonics boom like the thesis fell apart, and it did not. The orders are signed, and the factories are going up. Everyone got scared right as it got cheap. text:
Quantum Computing Inc. $QUBT just made one of its biggest strategic moves—and it’s about far more than buying another company.

The acquisition of NHanced Semiconductors signals that QUBT is shifting from being a company with promising quantum technology to one that wants control over manufacturing and commercialization.

That difference matters.

Many quantum companies have brilliant science but depend on third-party manufacturers to turn prototypes into real products. QUBT is trying to shorten that gap.

The deal is structured intelligently.

• $68.1M cash upfront
• $5M in QUBT stock
• Up to $72M in earnout payments only if NHanced achieves ambitious revenue and EBITDA targets during 2027-2028.
• $20M remains in escrow, protecting QUBT if performance milestones aren’t reached.

This isn’t simply buying technology.

It’s buying execution, while making sellers earn the highest valuation through future performance.

For long-term investors, that’s a positive signal.

NHanced brings semiconductor packaging, advanced integration, and manufacturing capabilities that could become critical as QUBT develops photonic quantum computing, quantum sensing, AI hardware, and integrated optical chips.

The bigger picture is even more interesting.

The future AI stack won’t just require better software—it will require new hardware.

Quantum computing, silicon photonics, optical interconnects, advanced semiconductor packaging, and AI accelerators are increasingly converging into one ecosystem.

Owning more of that value chain gives QUBT greater control over product quality, production timelines, margins, intellectual property, and customer relationships.

However, investors should remain realistic.

The acquisition alone doesn’t prove commercial success.

QUBT still needs to demonstrate that NHanced can generate meaningful revenue growth and that the combined business can manufacture products at scale.

The required financial statements and pro-forma results have not yet been released, so it’s still too early to fully judge whether the purchase price represents a bargain or an expensive bet.

Meanwhile, the recent criticism surrounding Microsoft’s topological quantum research serves as another reminder of how early this industry still is.

Scientific breakthroughs remain uncertain.

Engineering remains difficult.

Commercial adoption will likely take years.

That’s why companies with real manufacturing capabilities may eventually separate themselves from companies relying solely on research papers.

The next phase of quantum computing won’t be won by the company with the best theory.

It will likely be won by the company that can design, manufacture, scale, and deliver commercially viable quantum hardware.

QUBT is betting that controlling more of its manufacturing pipeline today will give it a stronger competitive position tomorrow.

text:
The biggest investing mistake?
Thinking the next decade will look like the last one.
While most investors chase quarterly headlines, the biggest wealth creators are often built around long-term themes that play out over years:
📷 AI Infrastructure
📷 Space
📷 Robotics
📷 Photonics
📷 Energy
📷 Quantum Computing
📷 Rare Earths
📷 Defense Technology
📷 Genomics
These aren’t just industries.
They’re some of the technologies reshaping how we work, communicate, travel, defend, power, and improve our world.
The companies leading these trends today may become the market leaders of tomorrow.
The question isn’t whether change is coming.
The question is: Which trends will create the most value over the next 10 years?
Which mega theme are you most bullish on? 📷
#Investing #GrowthStocks #StockMarket #FutureTech #LongTermInvesting

text:
#truefreefinance  (June 27.2026 )Robotics is quietly emerging as the next major wave in tech investing
Recent data from Andreessen Horowitz and PitchBook (March update) suggests that investment activity in robotics is accelerating, with both funding and deal flow continuing to rise.
What makes this trend particularly interesting is its connection to the broader AI infrastructure cycle.
Many of the same sectors benefiting from AI data center expansion are also positioned to play a role in robotics and humanoid systems:
• Memory (DRAM & NAND) → supporting on-device AI inference and storage
• Photonics & optics → enabling machine vision, LiDAR, and sensing systems
• AI compute infrastructure → powering autonomous decision-making in real-world environments
At the moment, most exposure is still indirect — through upstream suppliers or large tech companies investing heavily in robotics, such as Amazon and Tesla.
But the bigger question for investors is forward-looking:
If capital markets reopen strongly in 2026–2027, will we start seeing more pure-play robotics and humanoid companies coming to public markets?
If that happens, robotics could move from a niche theme to a full investment cycle of its own.
Not financial advice. Based on publicly available market data and industry trends.
#AI #Robotics #HumanoidRobots #Technology #Investing #FutureTech #StockMarket

text:
Nvidia quietly became the #1 data center Ethernet switch vendor — $2.1B revenue, 21.5% market share, up from 4% two years ago. IDC calls it 'one of the most significant vendor landscape shifts' in enterprise networking history. The networking business tripled to $15B.

The obvious read: Nvidia is diversifying beyond GPUs.

The more strategic read: Nvidia is identifying the next AI bottleneck before it fully materializes, then buying or building the solution before anyone else realizes it's a problem.

At million-GPU cluster scale, the binding constraint isn't compute or even memory anymore. It's the fabric connecting them. Copper interconnects hit hard walls on power, bandwidth density, and reach. GPU utilization tanks when all-reduce and collective communication across racks can't keep up.

Every cluster operator has experienced this: plenty of FLOPs, terrible effective throughput because the network is the bottleneck.

Nvidia saw this coming when it bought Mellanox for $6.9B in 2019. Spectrum-X now generates more revenue than all Ethernet networking peers combined, per Jensen. The company is vertically integrating end-to-end cluster fabric the same way it integrated CUDA into the software stack.

The next phase: photonics. Nvidia's CPO roadmap with TSMC COUPE is preparing for optical interconnects at the rack level. Bandwidth per AI rack goes from ~130 TB/s to multiples beyond that.

Here's the under-covered part: the photonics supply chain — lasers, silicon photonics foundries, fiber attach, packaging — is even earlier in its high-volume ramp than HBM was in 2024. The qualification cycles are longer, yields lower, and the ecosystem less mature.

The AI bottleneck narrative has shifted: compute → memory → networking → optics.

$15B in networking revenue is the market signaling the next constraint. The companies that solve the photonics supply chain will be the Micron and SK Hynix of the next cycle.

#Nvidia #Photonics #AIChips #Networking

At million-GPU cluster scale, the binding constraint isn't compute or even memory anymore. It's the fabric connecting them. Copper interconnects hit hard walls on power, bandwidth density, and reach. GPU utilization tanks when all-reduce and collective communication across racks can't keep up. text:
Retail is waiting for pure-play robotics IPOs, while institutional money is quietly locking up the raw physical supply chains today.

The vertical spike highlighted confirms a massive paradigm shift: capital is moving aggressively into physical, embodied AI. As the first pure-play humanoid exits open the liquidity floodgates for H2 into 2027, the structural allocation war we are seeing in data centers will expand exponentially into robotics.

The smart money is front-running this cross-exposure overlap through critical upstream hardware:
 The Memory Squeeze Phase II: A humanoid robot running dense, real-time localized inference models requires up to a 10x memory multiplier over regular edge hardware. The exact same HBM, DRAM, and NAND components currently supply-constrained through 2027 to feed hyperscalers are facing an entirely new, massive downstream demand sink.
 The Photonics Vision Choke Point: You cannot run autonomous, real-time spatial manipulation on a gutted hardware spec sheet. Next-gen FMCW LiDAR vision and sensor frameworks rely directly on high-power DFB laser chips. Companies like $AAOI or $LITE that control internal MOCVD crystal growth foundries aren't just building for transceivers—they are the literal gatekeepers of the physical optical sensing layer that every single humanoid platform requires to operate safely.

The Upstream Component Leverage: Retail is waiting for pure-play robotics IPOs to buy the hype, completely missing that the absolute pricing power sits with the upstream component layer right now. Whether the end-program is run by $TSLA , $AMZN or a venture-backed pure-play, they all must secure raw, physical capacity from the exact same tightly contracted component suppliers.

The cognition bottleneck has officially shifted to a physical execution bottleneck. When the factories are fully booked through mid-2027 just to satisfy data center demand, adding a multi-billion dollar humanoid hardware ramp into the equation means the choke points capture all the structural alpha.

Accumulating the physical plumbing before the global IPO season reprices the entire supply chain. Long the bottlenecks. 📈🤖🧠

text:
🧵 My personal market heuristic after tracking AI infrastructure themes closely:

Markets tend to price things in ~8 months in advance — except Space, Robotics, and Quantum, where sentiment runs well ahead of fundamentals.

Here's how I'm applying it across themes I follow: 👇

Photonics has a clear roadmap:

• 1.6T pluggables ( $LITE, $COHR, $AAOI) are hitting now
• CPO Scale-Out ramp: late H2 2026
• CPO Scale-Up (Ayar, Celestial, etc.): H2 2027

By the 8M rule, CPO Scale-Up names are still ~4 months early to position heavily.

Memory is mid-bottleneck and re-rating accordingly:

• HBM3e: now ($MU, Samsung, SK Hynix)
• HBM4 mass production: ramping now
• HBF: 2027

Same players across cycles = compounding opportunity as each architecture matures.

Glass Substrates:

• SKC Absolics early ramp: H2 2026
• Volume inflection: H2 2027–2028
• TSMC CoPoS, Samsung, others: 2027–2028

Machine/equipment suppliers here tend to see revenue before the volume ramp — worth noting for positioning.

Neoclouds: Q4 2026 earnings looks like a key inflection window for leading players.

In hindsight, March 2026 was the entry, not September 2025. The ~8M rule would've caught that.

Current repositioning focus:
• Memory
• Neoclouds
• Photonics
• Robotics

Preference for names capturing multiple themes simultaneously (e.g., pluggable + NPO + CPO exposure) and tactical exposure to bottleneck plays like advanced packaging and MLCCs.

Key personal lesson: Avoid heavy exposure to names 12M+ out. Early conviction is fine — overweighting too early is where sizing discipline matters most.

Still learning. These are just my own frameworks — not advice. 🙏

text:
Reflections in the Dip: Why My Conviction in $SIVE Has Never Been Stronger

I’ve been watching the recent pullback with a strange sense of calm.

Weak hands are panicking. That’s what they do when the price moves against them for a few weeks. But if you’ve been here before, you know this feeling isn’t new, it’s just the market doing what it always does to test conviction.

Over two years ago we were trading around 1.8 SEK. That drop felt brutal at the time. This one? This is noise compared to that. And yet the company is in a completely different place today.

We’re still in an industry that hasn’t really started yet. Photonics for AI data centers is still pre-ramp for most people. There are no shiny consumer products on shelves for the average investor to point at and say “that’s what I own.” No flashy earnings beats every quarter. Just deep technology, long qualification cycles, and a market that very few truly understand. Most people want to see something they can touch before they believe. That’s why these moves feel so violent, because the narrative isn’t obvious until it suddenly is.

That’s also why wireless has been so important. It’s been the quiet engine running in the background, delivering real revenue and building the foundation while photonics gets ready for its moment. Most of the market still doesn’t price that in properly. They’re too focused on the “when does photonics happen?” question and miss that the company is already executing on multiple fronts.

I expect photonics news and orders from Ayar Labs, $POET, O-Net, $AEVA and $JBL before the year is out. Those won’t just be headlines, they’ll be real validation that starts to de-risk the story and reduce some of this volatility. I’m 100% convinced of that. Not because I’m hoping. Because I’ve followed this company long enough to know how it works.

Nobody believes until it’s too late.

We saw it from 4 SEK to over 100 SEK. Almost nobody saw that coming when it was actually happening. The next leg won’t be any different.

There’s a lot of games being played right now, short sellers, institutions, traders all trying to take their piece. That’s normal when a stock has moved this much. It will stop when they’re done. Right now we’re also at a price level where a lot of serious money starts paying attention again. Don’t let the noise fool you.

Do your own work.

Read the presentations. Watch the CMDs. Look at the customer pipeline, the partnerships, the technology edge. Build your own picture instead of scrolling through what everyone else is saying.

If you don’t believe light is the future of high-speed connectivity, that’s fine. There are plenty of other sectors. But if you do understand the technology and what Sivers actually brings to the table, the differentiated InP platform, the asset-light model, the real design wins, then the choice becomes pretty obvious.

This is still a market that very few people truly grasp. A market that doesn’t even fully know how big it can get. And if you play it right, it rewards patience like few others.
I actually believe more in $SIVEF today than I did a couple of years ago.

Why? Because I can see the moves that are coming. Dual listing preparations are real. They’re not walking onto Nasdaq empty-handed. Watch what happens over the next few months. A lot of people are going to wish they had listened earlier.

For what it’s worth, I haven’t sold a single share. And I won’t. The day I sell is the day the company stops delivering. Not when the stock has a bad week. Not when the noise gets loud. Price and company are two different things.

This is still the early innings.

Stay calm. Do the work. Trust what you know.

Weak hands are panicking. That’s what they do when the price moves against them for a few weeks. But if you’ve been here before, you know this feeling isn’t new, it’s just the market doing what it always does to test conviction. Over two years ago we were trading around 1.8 SEK. That drop felt brutal at the time. This one? This is noise compared to that. And yet the company is in a completely different place today. We’re still in an industry that hasn’t really started yet. Photonics for AI data centers is still pre-ramp for most people. There are no shiny consumer products on shelves for the average investor to point at and say “that’s what I own.” No flashy earnings beats every quarter. Just deep technology, long qualification cycles, and a market that very few truly understand. Most people want to see something they can touch before they believe. That’s why these moves feel so violent, because the narrative isn’t obvious until it suddenly is. That’s also why wireless has been so important. It’s been the quiet engine running in the background, delivering real revenue and building the foundation while photonics gets ready for its moment. Most of the market still doesn’t price that in properly. They’re too focused on the “when does photonics happen?” question and miss that the company is already executing on multiple fronts. I expect photonics news and orders from Ayar Labs, $POET, O-Net, $AEVA and $JBL before the year is out. Those won’t just be headlines, they’ll be real validation that starts to de-risk the story and reduce some of this volatility. I’m 100% convinced of that. Not because I’m hoping. Because I’ve followed this company long enough to know how it works. Nobody believes until it’s too late. We saw it from 4 SEK to over 100 SEK. Almost nobody saw that coming when it was actually happening. The next leg won’t be any different. There’s a lot of games being played right now, short sellers, institutions, traders all trying to take their piece. That’s normal when a stock has moved this much. It will stop when they’re done. text:
ちょうど昨日Zackの記事もありました、GFSとUMCの投資ロジックの違いについて

1. 成長ドライバー：シリコンフォトニクス vs. 22nm成熟プロセス
 GlobalFoundries (GFS) —— 高成長なニッチ市場への注力：
GFSはターゲットを絞ったアプローチをとっており、AIやデータセンターのインフラ需要を直接取り込んでいます。特に、光通信向けのシリコンフォトニクス (Silicon Photonics) 技術が牽引しており、2026年には同事業の売上が倍増、2028年末にはランレートで10億ドルを突破する見込みです。また、高周波通信向けのシリコンゲルマニウム (SiGe) ターンキーソリューションは2027年までキャパシティが埋まっています。AI光通信チェーンや欧米のローカルサプライチェーンのテーマに投資したい場合、GFSのストーリーは非常に魅力的です。
 UMC (聯電) —— 規模の経済とコスト効率：
UMCは成熟プロセスの雄であり、主力である22nmプラットフォームが業績を支えています。2026年末までに50社以上の顧客がテープアウト（設計完了）を予定しており、ディスプレイ駆動ICやマイクロコントローラ（MCU）など、幅広い汎用チップを網羅しています。GFSのような派手なAIインフラのテーマ性は薄いものの、Intelと共同開発している12nmプロセスが2027年に量産開始予定であり、将来的に高付加価値市場への参入ロードマップを描いています。

2. 財務パフォーマンスとバリュエーション
直近の2026年第1四半期（Q1）決算では、両社ともに売上総利益率（グロス・マージン）を約29%に維持していますが、その中身は異なります。
 GFS（バリュエーションは高め、長期的価格支配力）： スマートフォン市場の減速によりモバイル部門が逆風を受けているものの、デザインウィン（設計獲得）が前年同期比で50%急増しています。これは顧客の囲い込みが順調である証拠であり、長期的な価格支配力の強さを示しています。
 UMC（稼働率回復による短期的な利益爆発力）： Q1の設備稼働率が79%（前年同期から10ポイント上昇）まで回復したことで、営業レバレッジが効き、純利益が大幅に急増しました。また、UMCは伝統的に配当が安定しており配当利回りが高いため、インカムゲインを重視する投資家にとっても安心感があります。

3. どちらが今日の優れた投資機会か？
投資スタイルやポートフォリオの戦略によって結論が分かれます。
📥 GlobalFoundries (GFS) を選ぶべきケース：
長期的なアルファ（市場平均を超える超過リターン）を狙う場合です。
主力であるモバイル向けビジネスが2026年後半まで高一桁の減少を見込むなど短期的には逆風がありますが、米国・ドイツ・シンガポールに分散された製造拠点は、地政学的リスクを嫌う大手クライアント（Appleやルネサスなど）のニーズに合致しています。プレミアムなマルチプル（株価倍率）を許容し、AIインフラや光ネットワークへの構造転換に賭けたい投資家向けです。
📥 UMC (聯電) を選ぶべきケース：
景気サイクル好転による安心感と、確実なキャッシュフローを狙う場合です。
現在のUMCの主な懸念点は、シンガポール工場の拡張に伴う巨額の減価償却費であり、これが2026年後半の利益率を圧迫するリスクがあります。しかし、世界的な自動車、産業、家電向けチップの需要が本格的に回復すると予想する場合、稼働率上昇がそのまま利益に直結する高い財務レバレッジと、下値リスクを抑える高配当（防衛力）を兼ね備えた、バランスの良い循環株として非常に優秀です。

text:
Q1 2026 just printed a record: $16.3B invested across ~492 deals in robotics & physical AI. Humanoids are eating a massive slice of that capital. 

Deal sizes in the hundreds of millions are now normal. The steep upward trend you highlighted is accelerating into deployment phase, not just demos.
The cross-exposure angle is one of the highest-conviction parts of this story:

Memory (DRAM/HBM + NAND): Humanoids need serious onboard inference + storage. Same AI data center stack that’s already in hyper-growth directly feeds edge humanoid brains.

Photonics & sensing (DFB lasers for FMCW LiDAR): This is the vision/perception layer that turns clunky robots into dexterous, safe collaborators. Overlap with advanced auto/robotics supply chains is real and underappreciated.

Right now most investable exposure is still upstream components or inside large-cap winners like $TSLA and $AMZN (warehouse deployments scaling today).
But the pure-play IPO/exit wave is no longer theoretical:

Tesla is converting Fremont Model S/X lines — Optimus V3 production ramp starts Summer 2026. Public sales targeted toward late 2027. This is the strongest signal yet that 2026 is the bridge year from prototype to early volume.

Unitree (China’s volume leader — already shipping thousands of humanoids/quadrupeds with ~60% gross margins) filed its STAR Market IPO in March targeting ~$7B valuation. First major liquidity event.

Agility Robotics (Digit bots already working with Amazon, GXO, etc.) is going public via SPAC soon (expected ticker $AGLT).

Global IPO season for pure-play humanoids & robotics platforms is kicking off in H2 2026 and runs hot into 2027
embedded_text:
Robotics is next. 

Both deal count and investment amounts are skyrocketing per pitchbook March data (source: a16z)

Good thing is: the same AI DC exposure often has cross-exposure to humanoid ramp.

text:
@OpenAI’s first custom chip, Jalapeño, built with @Broadcom for inference.

Everyone’s reading it as a shot at @nvidia. 

The deeper signal is that at this scale, the bottleneck isn’t just the compute, it’s the data moving between them.

Copper’s running out and Broadcom’s other big bet is the answer is photonics.

#photonics #copper #ai #openai

text:
Weekly portfolio update:

A rough week for the portfolio finishing
9.8% down 🔴

My photonics names $SIVE & $IQE took big hits this week: $SIVE down 29% & $IQE 7%.

Rolls Royce had a pretty flat week, down 0.14%🔴

All holdings in my pie finished red for the week too.

$VWRP -1.63%🔴
$SEMI -5.31%🔴
$QNTG -3.65%🔴

I crossed £2,000 last Friday, I’m now back under despite depositing £181 this week.

How was your week?👇

July Picks: Already Firing

$MU + $DRAM: Closing green. The AI memory trade remains strong. My view: $DRAM has a path to $100+ by late July or August if the memory rally continues.

$AAOI: $132 → $150

$OUST: $42 → $54

$IREN: Still the laggard. EOY target: $120+. July could see a move into the $70–80 range if momentum builds. ✅

$CRDO: I believe this is setting up for a move into the $300s. After the recent pullback from the low $300s, strong revenue growth, AI networking demand, and partnerships across the photonics ecosystem could drive another leg higher. ✅

*I am STILL adding more $CRDO & $IREN at these levels. 

Not financial advice. These are my personal view

text:
TSLA
surging 7% for its best day in 14 months.

OUST
surging on NDAA ban of Chinese LiDAR and Agility Robotics SPAC news

RKLB
acquiring Iridium transforms it into a vertically integrated space platform.

SMCI
🩸 offices raided in Taiwan over alleged chip smuggling to China.

AMAT
hit a new all-time high after dual analyst price target hikes.

PANW
broke out to a new 52-week high alongside the broader cybersecurity rally

GLW
breaking out to all-time highs as the photonics leader reclaims its throne.

ASTS
up 22% on Japan's expected $1B satellite-to-phone subsidy.

AMZN
may pay more for Anthropic after renegotiating to token-based pricing.

UPC
ripped 500% premarket after announcing $10.8M in China patents

text:
Disorder creates new properties in compound semiconductors - Helmholtz-Zentrum Berlin (HZB) https://t.co/YcmkNFjBWE

This first clear demonstration of phonon-exciton asymmetry opens doors to next-gen optoelectronics. The material pretends to be a bland cubic citizen but reveals a sophisticated, directionally picky inner life under light. Revolutionary for energy tech and photonics, disorder as deliberate design tool. Schorr’s team calls it “an additional handle for tailoring optoelectronic properties.”

Talk about a stick save  $LITE 

You got that potential U&R (Undercut and Recover) on the 100 day for 

Should be an interesting rest of week for this name and many other photonics names ex $GLW which is living in its own world.  

Have they shook out all the new comers and they are ready for a new leg?  Or is this Lucy waving the football at Charlie Brown just to pull it away. 

No one knows, but I'll have the 🍿 out

text:
8/11

This is where silicon photonics and micro-ring resonators become important.

A micro-ring can act like a tiny wavelength-selective filter.

It can help select, modulate, or separate specific wavelengths.

That makes WDM easier to integrate into photonic chips.

text:
This is the right take → attention relative to float is a fragility gauge, not a buy signal. The crowding z-score peaks right before the air pocket.

What's worth adding → it's symmetric, and your own example is confirming it in real time. The radar caught flow rotating OUT of photonics. Photonics is bouncing today. The same sensor that flagged crowding fragility on the way up flags abandonment on the way down → attention decays, the float stays pinned, and the setup reloads.

Crowding fragile, neglect cheap, same indicator read in two directions. Your Burry-SOXX fun fact is the purest version of that → peak query is peak neglect is the bottom. 👀

text:
$SIVE and $AAPL. Funny how a company named Rockley led us here.

A lot of you are asking if this connection is confirmed. Let me break it down.

Rockley Photonics was Apple's laser supplier for their glucose monitoring project. Apple poured $70 million into them.

Rockley went bankrupt in 2023.

So where does $AAPL go next?

Sivers Semiconductors is a small Swedish company that makes extremely precise lasers. So precise that they are the only laser technology in the world that can measure blood glucose through your skin without a needle.

That is not marketing. That is physics.

Here is what is officially confirmed.

In January 2024 $SIVE published a legal regulatory filing. The kind where executives face criminal charges if they lie.

It states that a US Fortune 100 company has been working with $SIVE for over 5 years. Paid $17 million in development fees. And just placed an order for 50,000 laser devices for a wearable health product.

That is real. That is in the filing.

The timing lines up. The technology fits. The unit volumes match.

$AAPL is never named in any $SIVE document. That part is still inference.

But if it is confirmed casually run these numbers.
Apple Watch ships up to 54 million units a year. At $10 to $15 per laser that is $500 to $750 million in potential annual revenue for $SIVE. Comparable sole source Apple suppliers have historically traded at 25 to 40 times sales at peak.

$SIVE sits at a $1.9 billion market cap today.
Rockley bankruptcy. $AAPL needs a new laser supplier. $SIVE announces a Fortune 100 wearable customer.

The confirmed volume order is the catalyst that changes everything.

text:
The easiest trade is following the crowd.

The hardest trade is buying a narrative before Wall Street believes it.

I’m betting on:

• Quantum
• Photonics
• AI
• Defense
• U.S. manufacturing

$QUBT

High risk.

Potentially asymmetric upside.

Time will tell.

text: Nokia/Infinera is a real position in the optical stack — but the market is still pricing it as a telecom story. The deeper opportunity is in silicon photonics and electro-optic modulators that eliminate the electrical-to-optical conversion bottleneck inside AI clusters entirely. Analyst downgrades miss the thesis shift.

text:
Funny how the market spent weeks arguing over $AXTI, $LITE and $IQE, while $ONTO quietly moved from about $287 to $345.

That is roughly a 20% move since I called it out, but the price move is not the main point.

The cleaner thesis was never “buy another photonics stock.” It was that co-packaged optics changes the cost of failure. In the old pluggable world, a bad part could be discarded. In CPO, a defect found after bonding can destroy a far more valuable assembly.

That pushes inspection upstream. It makes pre-bond metrology a gating step, not a support function.

So if $TSM, $NVDA and $AVGO are pushing the industry toward advanced optical packaging, the question is not only who supplies the light. It is who helps protect yield before the package becomes too expensive to fail.

That is why $ONTO mattered.

Not the flashiest name in photonics. Just one of the harder steps to bypass.

The stock moved first. The manufacturing constraint is still there.

While everyone is fighting over $AXTI, $LITE, and $IQE…

There’s a company quietly sitting at a chokepoint that NONE of them can function without.

$ONTO (Onto Innovation) — ~$14B market cap

Here’s why this is different from every other photonics stock people are talking about. https://t.co/bkSdRxFWq1
