"text": "While everyone is fighting over $AXTI, $LITE, and $IQE… There’s a company quietly sitting at a chokepoint that NONE of them can function without. $ONTO (Onto Innovation) — ~$14B market cap Here’s why this is different from every other photonics stock people are talking about. In the old pluggable world, if a chip failed during assembly, you threw away a $50 part. In the new CPO (Co-Packaged Optics) world — where light is fused permanently into the silicon — a single defect discovered AFTER bonding means scrapping a $10,000 assembly. That changes everything. Every CPO chip now has to be inspected at the wafer level BEFORE it ever gets permanently bonded. There is no bypass. There is no exemption. $ONTO’s Dragonfly G5 is the system doing that inspection. And in April 2026, it was officially qualified as the foundry standard for 2.5D advanced packaging. Think about what that means: - $TSMC runs COUPE (the CPO platform $NVDA and $AVGO are built on) - Every chip going into that platform needs pre-bonding optical inspection - The tool of record for that inspection just became Dragonfly G5 - There is no credible alternative at volume scale This is the “yield insurance” that makes the entire Glass City buildable. Without $ONTO, CPO doesn’t scale. The numbers back it up: - Q1 2026 revenue: $292M — beat guidance - Q2 2026 guidance: $320–$330M — 8% raise - Full year 2026 revenue projected to grow 30%+ - Advanced packaging and AI segments leading growth - 53.8% gross margins - Dragonfly G5: shipped, qualified, ramping And FinTwit is still arguing about InP substrates. $AXTI and $LITE get the attention because the thesis is simple to explain. $ONTO gets ignored because “wafer inspection” doesn’t sound sexy. But it is the last gate every single CPO chip must pass through before it goes live. The entire photonics supercycle runs through this company. And barely anyone is talking about it. ----- *Not financial advice. Do your own due diligence."}}}, "rest_id": "2054190271466729642", "source": "Twitter for iPhone", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "3493", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}}, "rest_id": "2071659851273429306", "source": "Twitter Web App", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "4860", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}

🔍 New patent application #US20260172716A1 by #Samsung introduces a solid-state imaging device featuring a pixel array with single-photon avalanche diodes (SPADs) and a counter unit.
The counter unit includes a data generation circuit and memory, enhancing photon counting precision.
Explore innovations in #ImagingTech and #Photonics with this #PatentApplication.

text:
Personally looking at Quantum Technology "RIGID" stocks

here's what I think overall

May 21st, $2 Billion+ was committed to the CHIPS and Science Act, plus two executive orders on June 22nd to reinforce this:
> quantum computing got DoE 
> migrate to post-quantum computing by 2030 by agencies

There are 9 companies in US stocks that this can affect directly

The RIGID stocks imo have the higher importance
Rigetti $RGTI 
IMB $IBM 
Global Foundations $GFS 
Infleqion $INFQ 
D-Wave $QBTS 

With these, the two that I have personal interest are IMB and GFS. 

IMB needs to introduction here and technically the money goes to Anderson, but its still the front runner of superconducting for 2028 and having domestic but indirect infrastructure is still a benefit to the company. 

GFS is focused on all quantum based architecture, but committed to AI Data Centers domestically, called SCALE for high bandwidth but energy efficient connectivity 

I have been looking for a company that will release the pressure valve on demand and GFS could do it in the same way that broadband helped de-pressurize the telecom days.

I am not as bullish on QBTS for example, I think they have too much rising debts and revenue fell 81%, but could get a partnership or acquisition. 

RGTI def worth a watch with their $NVDA NVQLink partnership and Cepheus launch 

The other 4 stocks worth mentioning:
Atom Computing 
DIraq
PsiQuantum 
Quantinnum 

These are mainly supply chain stocks for sensors and photonics but could be part of the discussion. They could certainly do well or bad, but I think the RIGIDs would help them rise, so better imo to focus on the main stack. 

Ive heard about quantum technology for a long time

Is it finally here?

Technology "RIGID" stocks here's what I think overall May 21st, $2 Billion+ was committed to the CHIPS and Science Act, plus two executive orders on June 22nd to reinforce this: > quantum computing got DoE > migrate to post-quantum computing by 2030 by agencies There are 9 companies in US stocks that this can affect directly The RIGID stocks imo have the higher importance Rigetti $RGTI IMB $IBM Global Foundations $GFS Infleqion $INFQ D-Wave $QBTS With these, the two that I have personal interest are IMB and GFS. IMB needs to introduction here and technically the money goes to Anderson, but its still the front runner of superconducting for 2028 and having domestic but indirect infrastructure is still a benefit to the company. GFS is focused on all quantum based architecture, but committed to AI Data Centers domestically, called SCALE for high bandwidth but energy efficient connectivity I have been looking for a company that will release the pressure valve on demand and GFS could do it in the same way that broadband helped de-pressurize the telecom days. I am not as bullish on QBTS for example, I think they have too much rising debts and revenue fell 81%, but could get a partnership or acquisition. RGTI def worth a watch with their $NVDA NVQLink partnership and Cepheus launch The other 4 stocks worth mentioning: Atom Computing DIraq PsiQuantum Quantinnum These are mainly supply chain stocks for sensors and photonics but could be part of the discussion. They could certainly do well or bad, but I think the RIGIDs would help them rise, so better imo to focus on the main stack. Ms. Tewari brings over 35 years of experience in corporate operations and manufacturing leadership—spanning the aerospace, defense, telecommunications, and advanced industrial sectors—with a focus on laser technology.

She has held senior leadership roles overseeing multi-site operations across North America and Europe, managing complex and highly regulated manufacturing activities at organizations such as Northrop Grumman, Raytheon, Comtech Telecommunications, and Pratt & Whitney.

In her role at Laser Photonics, Ms. Tewari will oversee global operations, leading manufacturing optimization and the scaling of enterprise processes to enhance productivity, quality, and delivery performance in support of sustainable growth.

"Ann joins us at an exciting time for the company," said Wayne Tupuola, CEO of Laser Photonics. "Following two major acquisitions that expanded our operations, we are now focused on refining our processes and aligning strategies across the organization."

text:
$IONQ is looking to become vertically integrated.

The acquisition of $SKYT seems like the missing piece.

It’s not.

$IONQ buys laser systems from Coherent and NKT Photonics.

Coherent is a US company that supplies lasers to $IONQ called Coherent Paladin.

The ultraviolet lasers produce light at specific wavelengths for the atomic transitions in barium and ytterbium that $IONQ uses.

Those are standard benchtop setups for general qubit manipulation. They’re massive and require lab environment to operate.

Then there’s NKT Photonics from Denmark. It supplies tailor made crystal fiber lasers with low noise. The goal here is to use the lasers in datacenters.

The setup is smaller so that the lasers can be mounted in a 19-inch rack with the other components of the quantum computer.

The lasers are ultra-stable and narrow-linewidth. It’s challenging to simply swap them with anyone else’s lasers.

Which makes $IONQ vulnerable to this specific supply chain.

Coherent relies on gallium, germanium, and rare-earth elements, most of which are mined in China. China suspended its total ban on exporting critical dual-use metals to the US until November 27, 2026.

What will happen after? Nobody knows.

We know from Coherent’s 2023 update that it had ample inventories and suppliers outside of China.

NKT Photonics sources nonlinear crystals used to build frequency converters from Europe, Japan, and the US.

The dangers here are regulations and delays.

NKT Photonics is a subsidiary of Hamamatsu Photonics. It has preferential access to Hamamatsu’s supply chain of laser diodes and optical components.

Trade wars between Europe and Japan are unlikely, but if they happen, NKT Photonics will be affected.

Finally, its lasers are dual use: They can be used in quantum computers and military guidance or radar systems.

Any sudden restrictions by Danish authorities could result in bottlenecks.

$IONQ is managing its supply chain nicely but dangers remain.

The acquisition of $SKYT seems like the missing piece. It’s not. $IONQ buys laser systems from Coherent and NKT Photonics. Coherent is a US company that supplies lasers to $IONQ called Coherent Paladin. The ultraviolet lasers produce light at specific wavelengths for the atomic transitions in barium and ytterbium that $IONQ uses. Those are standard benchtop setups for general qubit manipulation. They’re massive and require lab environment to operate. Then there’s NKT Photonics from Denmark. It supplies tailor made crystal fiber lasers with low noise. The goal here is to use the lasers in datacenters. The setup is smaller so that the lasers can be mounted in a 19-inch rack with the other components of the quantum computer. The lasers are ultra-stable and narrow-linewidth. It’s challenging to simply swap them with anyone else’s lasers. Which makes $IONQ vulnerable to this specific supply chain. Coherent relies on gallium, germanium, and rare-earth elements, most of which are mined in China. China suspended its total ban on exporting critical dual-use metals to the US until November 27, 2026. What will happen after? Nobody knows. We know from Coherent’s 2023 update that it had ample inventories and suppliers outside of China. NKT Photonics sources nonlinear crystals used to build frequency converters from Europe, Japan, and the US. The dangers here are regulations and delays. NKT Photonics is a subsidiary of Hamamatsu Photonics. It has preferential access to Hamamatsu’s supply chain of laser diodes and optical components. Trade wars between Europe and Japan are unlikely, but if they happen, NKT Photonics will be affected. Finally, its lasers are dual use: They can be used in quantum computers and military guidance or radar systems. Any sudden restrictions by Danish authorities could result in bottlenecks. $IONQ is managing its supply chain nicely but dangers remain."}}}, "rest_id": "2072033679514468359", "source": "Twitter Web Client", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "4113", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}

text:
Thread

Everyone keeps asking the same question.

"Which company wins AI?" 
$NVDA $AVGO $AMD $TSM $MU $MSFT $GOOGL $AMZN $META $ORCL $PLTR ?

I think that's the wrong question.

The better question is:

Which company becomes impossible to replace?

The AI infrastructure buildout isn't being constrained by GPUs anymore.

It's becoming constrained by everything around them.

Power.

Cooling.

Memory.

Networking.

And increasingly...

Light.

Most investors still think optical networking is about transceivers.

It isn't.

The real value is quietly moving upstream.

Every new optical architecture—whether it's pluggables, Scale-Out CPO, Scale-Up CPO, Near-Packaged Optics, or External Laser Source (ELS)—still depends on one thing:

A stable, high-volume CW laser.

Different architectures.

Same dependency.

That's where supply-chain mapping becomes interesting.

This is why I continue spending far more time studying suppliers than hyperscalers.

Hyperscalers change vendors.

Platforms evolve.

Chip architectures change.

But infrastructure bottlenecks?

Those usually last much longer than people expect.

The companies solving those bottlenecks often capture the highest operating leverage of the entire cycle.

This is exactly why I find $SIVE so interesting.

Most people still describe Sivers as "a laser company."

I think that's missing the point.

The product isn't the thesis.

Its position in the supply chain is.

If multiple AI networking architectures all require the same laser technology, then the value isn't coming from any single customer.

It's coming from becoming embedded across the ecosystem.

That's a completely different investment framework.

People also underestimate how infrastructure cycles actually work.

Revenue is always the last thing to arrive.

First comes R&D.

Then qualification.

Then design wins.

Then ecosystem validation.

Only after all of that do production ramps begin.

Investors waiting for obvious revenue growth are usually arriving after the market has already repriced the opportunity.

One thing I think the market still misunderstands is timing.

Many investors expect AI photonics to become a meaningful revenue driver this quarter.

I don't.

The real story is what happens when next-generation optical deployments begin scaling throughout 2027.

That's when supply-chain positioning starts to matter far more than quarterly headlines.

I've said this before:

I don't invest by asking,

"Who sells the most AI chips?"

I ask,

"Who does everyone else quietly depend on?"

Those companies rarely make headlines.

But they often become the biggest winners of the cycle.

That's why I continue mapping bottlenecks instead of narratives.

NFA. DYODD.@aleabitoreddit #StockMarket  #Investing #AI

"Which company wins AI?" $NVDA $AVGO $AMD $TSM $MU $MSFT $GOOGL $AMZN $META $ORCL $PLTR ? I think that's the wrong question. The better question is: Which company becomes impossible to replace? The AI infrastructure buildout isn't being constrained by GPUs anymore. It's becoming constrained by everything around them. Power. Cooling. Memory. Networking. And increasingly... Light. Most investors still think optical networking is about transceivers. It isn't. The real value is quietly moving upstream. Every new optical architecture—whether it's pluggables, Scale-Out CPO, Scale-Up CPO, Near-Packaged Optics, or External Laser Source (ELS)—still depends on one thing: A stable, high-volume CW laser. Different architectures. Same dependency. That's where supply-chain mapping becomes interesting. This is why I continue spending far more time studying suppliers than hyperscalers. Hyperscalers change vendors. Platforms evolve. Chip architectures change. But infrastructure bottlenecks? Those usually last much longer than people expect. The companies solving those bottlenecks often capture the highest operating leverage of the entire cycle. This is exactly why I find $SIVE so interesting. Most people still describe Sivers as "a laser company." I think that's missing the point. The product isn't the thesis. Its position in the supply chain is. If multiple AI networking architectures all require the same laser technology, then the value isn't coming from any single customer. It's coming from becoming embedded across the ecosystem. That's a completely different investment framework. People also underestimate how infrastructure cycles actually work. Revenue is always the last thing to arrive. First comes R&D. Then qualification. Then design wins. Then ecosystem validation. Only after all of that do production ramps begin. Investors waiting for obvious revenue growth are usually arriving after the market has already repriced the opportunity. One thing I think the market still misunderstands is timing. Many investors expect AI photonics to become a meaningful revenue driver this quarter. I don't. The real story is what happens when next-generation optical deployments begin scaling throughout 2027. That's when supply-chain positioning starts to matter far more than quarterly headlines. I've said this before: I don't invest by asking, "Who sells the most AI chips?" I ask, "Who does everyone else quietly depend on?" Those companies rarely make headlines. But they often become the biggest winners of the cycle. That's why I continue mapping bottlenecks instead of narratives. NFA. text:
💰 Nyemissioner - positivt eller negativt? Allt handlar om syftet.

Jag har märkt att många småsparare och nya investerare automatiskt kopplar ordet nyemission till något negativt och därför blir oroliga.

Det är fullt förståeligt – många nyemissioner genomförs när bolag har ekonomiska problem.

Men det betyder inte att alla nyemissioner ser likadana ut. I detta fallet handlar det dessutom om en riktad nyemission som riktas direkt mot större institutionella investerare och kommer dessutom genomföras över natten.

⸻

I Sivers, $SIVE, fall är budskapet från bolaget tydligt:

Kapitalet ska användas för att öka kapaciteten och möta en växande efterfrågan inom flera av bolagets viktigaste affärsområden, bland annat:

✅ Optiska lasrar och optiska förstärkare för AI-datacenter

✅ Automotive LiDAR

✅ Fortsatt expansion inom Photonics

Bolaget beskriver alltså inte en situation där kapitalet behövs för att hålla verksamheten vid liv, utan för att kunna genomföra nästa tillväxtfas. Det är en viktig skillnad!

⸻

Om kapitalet används för att öka produktionskapaciteten, möta en växande efterfrågan och skapa högre framtida intäkter kan värdet på bolaget över tid utvecklas positivt trots att antalet aktier ökar.

Det är just därför syftet med en nyemission är minst lika viktigt som nyemissionen i sig.

⸻

Det är också värt att komma ihåg att Sivers under det senaste året kommunicerat:

🔹 En växande pipeline inom AI-datacenter.

🔹 Produktionsupptrappning inom Automotive LiDAR från H2 2026.

🔹 Flera planerade produktionsramper under 2026–2027.

Det är mot den bakgrunden jag tycker att den här nyemissionen ska bedömas.

Personligen ser jag denna nyemissionen har ett väldigt starkt signalvärde kring vart bolaget är på väg!

⸻

Detta är inte en köp- eller säljrekommendation.

Jag tycker bara att det är viktigt att läsa hela pressmeddelandet och förstå varför bolaget tar in kapital – inte bara reagera på ordet nyemission!

text:
$SIVE is being mispriced because the market is still reading it as a small photonics hardware vendor.

That framing is outdated.

The real position is inside the AI optical stack, at a specific constraint layer: the laser source.

AI networking stack is shifting fast:

GPU clusters
→ scale-out bandwidth demand
→ 800G to 1.6T optics transition
→ CPO / LPO architecture shift
→ laser source layer becomes the constraint
→ InP / EML / CW laser ecosystem

Sivers sits in that lower layer.

Not as a module assembler.

But closer to the optical source and integration bottleneck that determines whether higher-level optics can actually scale.

The key shift the market is missing:

AI is no longer primarily a compute scaling problem.

It is becoming a data movement problem.

And data movement is limited by optical physics, not silicon performance.

Three structural points:

First, bandwidth demand in AI clusters is accelerating faster than electrical interconnect scaling.

Second, 1.6T and CPO architectures increase dependency on stable, high-quality laser sources.

Third, once optical components are designed into a platform, replacement cycles are long and sticky.

This changes how SIVE should be viewed.

Not as a cyclical supplier tied to component demand.

But as a potential design-in layer in optical reference architectures.

The bull case is not near-term revenue growth.

It is architectural adoption.

If Sivers becomes embedded in next-generation optical designs, demand becomes structural across hyperscaler buildouts.

Risks remain significant:

early-stage business model
historical dilution
customer concentration
execution risk in scaling photonics manufacturing

This is still a high-volatility setup.

But the key re-rating driver is simple:

Control points in AI systems are shifting from compute to connectivity, and from connectivity to optical physics.

That is where Sivers sits.@aleabitoreddit 
#StockMarket  #Investing

text:
$ALMU $ALMU continues building the team needed to move from R&D to commercial production.

The company appointed Brendan Moran, Ph.D. as Vice President of Engineering, a key hire as Aeluma prepares to scale manufacturing of its compound semiconductor technology.

Moran brings 18 years at Lumileds, where he led product strategy and development for a mobile flash business generating over $300 million in annual revenue. He also oversaw the launch of 25+ products into high-volume manufacturing, producing more than 4 billion semiconductor devices, and managed engineering teams responsible for taking products from development to mass production.

Why this matters:

Developing breakthrough semiconductor technology is only half the challenge. The harder part is turning lab-scale innovation into reliable, high-volume manufacturing that customers can deploy. That’s exactly where Moran’s experience fits.

For Aeluma, the focus is now shifting from proving its technology to commercializing it across AI infrastructure, optical communications, defense, aerospace, quantum computing, AR/VR, automotive and advanced sensing.

Aeluma’s platform integrates high-performance compound semiconductors with standard silicon manufacturing, aiming to deliver the performance advantages of III-V materials while using scalable semiconductor production processes. If successful, this could lower manufacturing costs while enabling broader adoption in fast-growing AI and photonics markets.

For investors, this is more than a management hire. It’s another signal that Aeluma is preparing for its next phase—moving beyond R&D and positioning itself for commercial revenue, manufacturing scale, and customer deployment.

The contract wasn't just another SBIR grant. It was a Direct-to-Phase II award, meaning Aeluma had already demonstrated enough technical progress to bypass Phase I. That's a much higher bar than simply winning research funding. What really caught my attention, though, was the solicitation itself. The Army isn't asking for a niche military prototype it wants an optical amplifier platform scalable enough for future quantum computing, directed-energy systems and quantum sensing. Even more interesting, the Phase III roadmap explicitly points toward telecom and LiDAR, meaning the government is helping fund technology that overlaps with Aeluma's commercial markets. To me, that's the bigger story.
